ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific grilled chicken salad entry; closest match is the general chicken salad food product class (FOODON_00002809). Grilled preparation and Caesar-style dressing are not distinguished at this level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated grilled chicken salad code; A0CKM (composite salad dish with meat) is the closest defensible anchor. Specific ingredients (avocado, olives, croutons, Caesar dressing) are not individually coded at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps directly to grilled chicken salad as a food item in the standard clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled chicken salad is approximately 250 g, representing one restaurant-style bowl containing grilled chicken breast, mixed greens, egg, avocado, olives, croutons, and Caesar dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Chicken Salad — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Grilled Chicken Salad (cr_id F14080015) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled chicken breast salad with dressing (NDB No. 35188) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients are set to -1 as they are not reported in the HPB source for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed green salad with protein (GI ~15)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled chicken salad with Caesar dressing. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on the analogous food category of mixed green salad with lean protein from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are croutons and vegetables; the overall carbohydrate content is very low at 2.32 g/100g, yielding a glycemic load per 250 g serving of approximately 0.87 g, which is negligible. This estimate is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium at 78 mg/100g; potassium-rich ingredients (avocado, lettuce) support DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Grilled chicken salad is suitable for hypertension management given its very low sodium content (78 mg/100g); patients should request dressing on the side or substitute Caesar dressing with a vinegar-based option to avoid sodium additions from commercial dressings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.32 g/100g), low sugar (1.8 g/100g), and estimated GI of 15 produce negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled chicken salad is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate content, high protein (6.64 g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.39 g/100g), which together minimise postprandial blood glucose rise; patients should avoid sugary dressings and limit crouton portions to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g/100g), lean grilled protein, and heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from avocado and olive oil dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled chicken salad supports cardiovascular health through its lean protein source, low saturated fat content, and inclusion of avocado which provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids; patients should choose olive oil-based dressing over Caesar dressing to minimise saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory profile: low saturated fat, dietary fibre from mixed greens, polyphenols from olive oil dressing
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled chicken salad is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile; patients should use extra-virgin olive oil-based dressing to maximise oleocanthal benefit and avoid croutons to reduce refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Chicken Salad is a Western-style composite salad dish consisting of grilled chicken breast served over a bed of fresh lettuce, accompanied by hard-boiled or sliced egg, creamy avocado, olives, and crunchy croutons, typically dressed with Caesar dressing. The chicken breast is grilled to produce a lightly charred, tender, and juicy protein component with minimal added fat. The salad delivers a satisfying contrast of textures — crisp lettuce, creamy avocado, chewy olives, and crunchy croutons — with a savoury, umami-rich flavour from the Caesar dressing. Nutritionally, this dish is low in calories (59 kcal/100g), low in carbohydrates (2.32 g/100g), and provides a meaningful protein contribution (6.64 g/100g) alongside dietary fibre (1.39 g/100g) from the vegetable components. Sodium is low at 78 mg/100g from the base dish, though dressing additions can significantly increase sodium. The dish is clinically relevant for blood glucose management (very low GI ~15, negligible glycaemic load), hypertension management (low sodium, DASH-compatible), and cardiovascular health (lean protein, avocado monounsaturated fats). It is widely consumed as a lunch or light dinner option in Western and international restaurant settings, and is popular in Singapore's café and casual dining scene. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and dressing. Common allergens include egg, gluten from croutons, and fish and dairy from Caesar dressing.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match entry (cr_id F14080015). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy for a closely analogous grilled chicken salad entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables, which is the best available approved source for this composite dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 35188, chicken salad with dressing) as HPB does not report this field. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish entry, and detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture breakdowns for this specific preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 3. The input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based', which are incorrect for a dish containing grilled chicken breast and egg; these tags have been removed. The dish is classified as non-vegetarian and non-vegan. 4. GI value of 0 in the input record has been corrected to an analogous estimate of 15 (low) based on approved GI sources; a GI of 0 is not defensible for a dish containing croutons and vegetables. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 5.8) / 100 = 0.87 g, where carbohydrate per 250g serving = 2.32 × 2.5 = 5.8 g. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for grilled chicken salad with Caesar dressing as a composite dish.
